message:
test(codec): cross-version conformance tests for additive-end vs middle-insert (ft-kuxho.B.2)

Step 2 of the rolling-upgrade window rollout. Extends the ft-e1emx
custom_pdu_conformance_matrix with two synthetic-future-PDU tests
that bind the version-pair semantics from ft-kuxho.B.1's check_compat
to the wire-format properties the proposal depends on.

cross_version_additive_end_of_struct_decodes_canonically (positive):
  - calls check_compat(local=v+1, local_min=v, remote=v, remote_min=v)
    and asserts the window agrees on v
  - encodes a SetLayoutCycle PDU with a Vec<String> tail field
  - appends three synthetic future-field byte sequences (4-byte, 1-byte,
    32-byte) after the canonical frame
  - asserts every appended variant decodes back to the canonical PDU
    with the same serial — the load-bearing property that makes
    additive bumps rolling-upgrade safe.

cross_version_middle_insert_does_not_silently_decode_canonically (negative):
  - same compat-window setup as the positive test
  - encodes a SetLayoutCycle PDU
  - inserts a 0xAB byte at every interior position (1..len-1) and
    asserts that EACH position surfaces as either decode-error or
    a decoded-but-non-equal PDU
  - the forbidden outcome is "decode succeeds AND result == canonical
    PDU", which would mean a positional drift was silently absorbed.
    A sanity assertion confirms every interior position contributed
    to the detect-or-error count (no silent equalities).

2/2 new tests pass. Full conformance suite (1 matrix + 1 Option-tag-byte
guard + 5 check_compat + 2 cross_version = 9 tests) green.

Closes ft-kuxho.2.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-c17iw","title":"semantic_anomaly::{Semantic,Conformal}AnomalyDetector::observe allows NaN/Inf embeddings to permanently poison centroid","description":"Both observe() methods normalize the input embedding via normalize_simd, which returns the input unchanged when mag_sq is NaN (NaN > 0.0 is false). A single NaN or Inf component in the embedding flows through normalize → dot_product → distance (masked to 0.0 by f32::max) but the raw normalized_emb is then fed into the EWMA centroid update (mul_add). NaN propagates into every centroid component, renormalize preserves the NaN (because normalize_simd also fails closed to passthrough), and every subsequent observe() sees similarity=NaN → distance=0.0 → anomaly detection silently disabled forever. Fix: reject embeddings with any non-finite component at observe() entry (before centroid mutation). Matches the fail-closed pattern of ft-b4l62 (Histogram), ft-yskcu (continuous_backpressure), ft-0p5q5 (EValueMonitor::observe).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:54.255558Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:28:39.706659Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:28:39.706383Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-c1lfg","title":"[mcp] wa.mission_state mission_state filter miss still returns a success mission payload","description":"In crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:3696-3725, wa.mission_state checks params.mission_state and, when the active mission lifecycle state does not match, still returns McpEnvelope::success with the mission identity, lifecycle_state, counters, and assignments: [] rather than signaling a filter miss. The filter therefore behaves like a placeholder empty-result shim instead of a real selection gate.\n\nImpact: callers asking for a specific lifecycle state receive a success payload for a non-matching mission and must reverse-engineer the mismatch from the returned lifecycle_state field.  {"id":"ft-cbeyl","title":"[MEDIUM] mcp_manifest_golden db-gated tool whitelist silently drifts when tool renamed or removed","description":"In commit 1bde42b3 (ft-nnfwn: freeze no-db mcp manifest surface),
\`mcp_manifest_matches_golden_without_db\` at
crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_manifest_golden.rs:254-316 hard-codes
three whitelists of tools/resources/templates expected to be db-gated:

\`\`\`rust
for expected_absent_tool in [
    \"wa.accounts\",
    \"wa.events\",
    \"wa.reserve\",
    \"wa.release\",
    \"wa.search\",
    \"wa.send\",
    \"wa.workflow_run\",
] {
    assert!(
        !no_db_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"db-gated tool unexpectedly advertised without db_path: {expected_absent_tool}\"
    );
}
\`\`\`

The assertion only checks absence from \`no_db_tools\`. If a tool is
renamed (e.g., \`wa.accounts\` → \`wa.list_accounts\`) or removed entirely,
the whitelist entry silently stays true (\`!no_db_tools.contains(\"wa.accounts\")\`
is trivially true when \`wa.accounts\` no longer exists in either manifest).

The same drift pattern applies to the resource whitelist
(lines ~285, \`wa://accounts\`, \`wa://events\`, \`wa://reservations\`) and
the template whitelist (lines ~295, \`wa://accounts/{service}\` etc.).

Impact: the no-db subset guard gives false confidence. A tool could
accidentally become always-on (or be removed from db-gated registration)
without the test catching it.

Fix: for each whitelist entry, assert \`full_tools.contains(entry)\`
BEFORE checking \`!no_db_tools.contains(entry)\`. That way, if the
whitelisted tool disappears from the db-backed manifest, the test
fails and forces whoever made the change to update the whitelist
deliberately.

\`\`\`rust
for expected_absent_tool in [...] {
    assert!(
        full_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"whitelist drift: {expected_absent_tool} no longer exists in db-backed manifest — update the test\"
    );
    assert!(
        !no_db_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"db-gated tool unexpectedly advertised without db_path: {expected_absent_tool}\"
    );
}
\`\`\`

Severity rationale: medium — test-quality issue that masks regressions
in MCP tool surface categorization. Not a correctness bug in production
code.

Evidence: commit 1bde42b3 diff lines 262-316 of the new
\`mcp_manifest_matches_golden_without_db\` test body.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:24:49.960054Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:03:23.915565Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:03:23.915363Z","close_reason":"Added full_*.contains(X) pre-assert for each whitelisted tool/resource/template before the !no_db.contains(X) check. If a whitelisted identifier disappears from the db-backed manifest, the test now fails instead of passing trivially. Fix landed in 4d52f550. Verified 6/6 tests pass via rch exec.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","review","test-gap","testing"]}

Cx-initiated cancellation (Error::Cancelled) therefore pollutes the circuit breaker's consecutive_failures counter — an external cancel can push the breaker past failure_threshold and reject subsequent unrelated requests with CircuitOpen.  {"id":"ft-gofz5","title":"[EPIC] Release engineering: first tagged release, cargo install validation, and binary distribution pipeline","description":"# Goal
Establish a working release pipeline so that \`cargo install --git ... ft\` actually works, release binaries are available for major platforms, and there are tagged versions users can reference.

# Background
The reality check found:
- Only 1 git tag exists ("backup-before-rewrite") — no version releases
- \`cargo install\` is advertised in README Quick Install but never validated
- 6 GitHub Actions workflows exist (ci.yml, release.yml, etc.) but release.yml has never been triggered for a real release
- The project is pre-1.0 with 4,288 commits but zero releases
- Build is resource-intensive (~793k LOC core library) which may cause cargo install to fail on memory-constrained machines

# Why This Matters
A project that can't be installed by its advertised method has zero users. Even for internal/swarm use, tagged releases let agents pin to known-good versions. Release hygiene signals project maturity.

# Architectural Intent
1. Validate that \`cargo install --git ... ft\` succeeds on a clean machine (Linux x86_64, macOS arm64 at minimum).
2. Create v0.1.0 tag with honest release notes reflecting current capabilities.
3. Ensure release.yml workflow produces downloadable binaries.
4. Add a CI job that runs \`cargo install\` from git to catch install regressions.

# Acceptance Criteria
1. \`cargo install --git https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm.git ft\` succeeds on Linux x86_64 and macOS arm64.
2. A v0.1.0 tag exists with release notes that honestly describe working features, partial features, and known limitations.
3. release.yml produces downloadable binaries for at least Linux x86_64 and macOS arm64.
4. CI includes a cargo install smoke test that runs on every push to main.
5. README Quick Install section includes minimum system requirements (RAM, disk, Rust nightly version).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:55:35.783442Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-11T02:07:18.344985Z","closed_at":"2026-04-11T02:07:18.344593Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","distribution","goal-line","release"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-gofz5","depends_on_id":"ft-d0ez0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:59:00.127254Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-gofz5.1","title":"Validate cargo install from git on clean Linux x86_64 and macOS arm64 environments","description":"# Goal
Prove that the advertised Quick Install command actually works on fresh machines.

# Design
1. On a clean Linux x86_64 environment (CI runner or fresh container):
   - Install Rust nightly (matching rust-toolchain.toml)
   - Run: cargo install --git https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/frankenterm.git ft
   - Verify: ft --version produces output
   - Verify: ft doctor runs without crash
   - Record: peak RAM usage, disk usage, wall time

2. On macOS arm64 (CI runner or local):
   - Same steps as above
   - Additional: verify LLVM/clang dependency resolution (cc alias issue documented in MEMORY.md)

3. If install fails, diagnose and fix:
   - Likely issues: memory exhaustion (793k LOC), missing system deps, cc alias conflict
   - Fix in Cargo.toml, build.rs, or CI configuration
   - Document minimum requirements

# Acceptance Criteria
1. cargo install succeeds on both platforms with exit code 0.
2. ft --version and ft doctor both work post-install.
3. Peak RAM and disk requirements documented.
4.  {"id":"ft-gofz5.2","title":"Create v0.1.0 tagged release with honest release notes and downloadable binaries","description":"# Goal
Cut the first real version release with honest release notes.

# Design
1. Version bump: Set version = "0.1.0" in relevant Cargo.toml files
2. Write release notes that honestly categorize:
   - Working: observation pipeline, pattern detection, Robot Mode, FTS search, policy engine, fleet memory, tiered scrollback, BOCPD, session persistence, connector SDK
   - Feature-gated: MCP, distributed mode, semantic search, web API, TUI
   - Partial: tx execution (orchestration framework complete, data plane in progress), mission dispatch
   - Known limitations: resource-intensive build, Rust nightly required, WezTerm backend dependency
3. Trigger release.yml workflow
4. Verify downloadable binaries are produced for Linux x86_64 and macOS arm64

# Acceptance Criteria
1. Git tag v0.1.0 exists with annotated release notes.
2. GitHub Release page has downloadable binaries.
3. Release notes are honest — no feature is listed as working unless it passes end-to-end.
4.  {"id":"ft-xv561","title":"[HIGH] pattern-engine regex construction has no backtrack limit — workspace-local ReDoS via user packs","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #2 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
The pattern engine compiles user-supplied regex patterns via
`fancy_regex::Regex::new(raw)` with NO backtrack limit or match
timeout. An attacker who can drop a YAML/JSON pattern pack into
`~/.config/wa/patterns/` or a repo's `.ft/patterns/` directory can
ship a catastrophic-backtracking regex that hangs `ft watch` the
first time matching pane output triggers the pattern's anchor.

## Evidence
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:13
  `use fancy_regex::Regex;`
- patterns.rs:512 (RuleDef::validate_regex)
  `Regex::new(regex).map_err(...)` — no `.backtrack_limit(...)`.
- patterns.rs:780 (build_engine_index)
  `let regex = Regex::new(raw).map_err(...)` — same.
- patterns.rs:920 (load_packs_from_config) + :935
  (load_pack_from_id) — pack files are loaded from
  `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` (default
  `~/.config/wa/patterns/`) AND from `.ft/patterns/` inside the
  resolved workspace root. The latter is an attacker-controlled
  directory when a repo is cloned from an untrusted source.
- mcp.rs:2010 (pattern-engine matching hot path)
  invokes `regex.captures_iter(text)` on every segment for every
  rule whose anchor matched. A single-threaded ReDoS blocks the
  matching worker; fancy_regex's default backtracking engine has
  no internal budget.

## Attack vector
1. attacker commits `.ft/patterns/evil.json` with a rule like
   ```json
   {
     "id": "evil.dos",
     "agent_type": "wezterm",
     "event_type": "noop",
     "anchors": ["a"],
     "regex": "^(a+)+$"
   }
   ```
2. user clones repo and runs `ft watch`.
3. the first pane output containing `"aaa...aaab"` (any near-match
   of the anchor) triggers captures_iter, which enters catastrophic
   backtracking. The pattern worker hangs indefinitely, wedging the
   detection pipeline.

No code execution — DoS only. But `ft watch` is the primary
operator surface; wedging the detection loop silently destroys
the "intelligent detection" claim the README makes.

## Fix direction
Two layers:

1. Compilation-time gate (quick):
   - Replace `Regex::new(raw)` with `fancy_regex::RegexBuilder::new(raw).backtrack_limit(1_000_000).build()`
     at patterns.rs:512 and :780. 1M steps is plenty for any
     legitimate rule and caps worst-case match cost.
   - Propagate the resulting `fancy_regex::Error::RuntimeError` from
     `captures_iter` as a `Detection` of kind `RegexRuntimeError`
     instead of hanging.

2. Match-time budget (robust):
   - Wrap each `regex.captures_iter(text)` call in a time-bounded
     harness (e.g., spawn a short-lived asupersync task with a
     100ms timeout). On timeout, quarantine the offending rule and
     surface a "rule disabled due to catastrophic match cost"
     telemetry event so operators can triage.

## Severity rationale
- HIGH (P1): attacker-controlled repo → DoS on user's machine.
- Not CRITICAL: local exploitation only (repo-clone vector), no
  network reach, no data exfil, no code execution.

## Out of scope for this bead
- RE2/Hyperscan migration (much larger rework).
- Per-rule memory budget (separate concern).

## Related
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (30988e53) — same audit pass
  already fixed the MCP arbitrary-file-read vector.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #2; recommend shipping the
compilation-time gate within one follow-up session.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:04:22.269417Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976852Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:14:08.976108Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 070117a0: fix(patterns): cap regex backtracking at 10M steps to close ft-xv561 pattern-engine ReDoS on attacker-controlled pattern packs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","patterns","redos","security"]}

 {"id":"ft-xydxz","title":"[review][LOW] ft-eze48 ftvi fuzz corpus has only one seed (FTVI magic + EOF), sparse coverage for libfuzzer starting points","description":"commit 6567397b landed fuzz/fuzz_targets/ftvi_index_from_bytes.rs with a single corpus seed at fuzz/corpus/ftvi_index_from_bytes/short_magic. Hex dump confirms it is 5 bytes: 46 54 56 49 0a (the real magic b'FTVI' followed by 0x0a).

The seed uses the correct magic bytes (verified against crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/vector_index.rs:10 const MAGIC = b'FTVI'). That is the good part — it seeds libfuzzer at the real format boundary, not random garbage.

Gap: only ONE seed, covering only ONE failure shape (magic-matches-but-truncated-at-version). A properly-seeded fuzz target typically ships 5-10 seeds exercising:
- Empty input (zero bytes)
- Minimal valid FTVI: magic + version=1 + dimension=0 + count=0 (12 bytes total, no records)
- Single-record FTVI: magic + version=1 + dimension=1 + count=1 + one (u64 id, u16 f16-value) record
- Magic-only (4 bytes — truncates before version)
- Valid magic + valid version + truncated at dimension (6 bytes)
- Valid header claiming count=1 + zero record bytes (parser must not OOB)
- Valid header + bogus version (e.g., version=255) to exercise version rejection

The Structured/Arbitrary lane at :66-84 generates real FTVI buffers at runtime via write_ftvi_vec, so coverage CAN be reached, but libfuzzer's coverage-guided exploration benefits from diverse starting seeds — especially for the header state machine, where coverage-guided mutation is more productive when seeded at valid-header boundaries.

Fix: add 5-7 curated seed files to fuzz/corpus/ftvi_index_from_bytes/ constructed via write_ftvi_vec and dd/printf. One-time scripting cost.

Severity: LOW — harness works as-is; this is a corpus-quality improvement, not a correctness gap. Audit round 14, 2026-04-23.

 {"id":"ft-y9lnb","title":"[EPIC] Transaction execution data-plane: connect orchestration to real pane operations, policy gates, and agent dispatch","description":"# Goal
Bridge the gap between FrankenTerm's well-engineered tx/mission orchestration framework and actual execution against live panes. Today the framework provides type-safe state machines, idempotency ledgers, observability hooks, and resume semantics — but step execution against panes is synthetic. This epic wires the data plane so that ft tx run actually does things.

# Background (reality check 2026-04-06, updated after three verification passes)
- tx_execution.rs (1,663 lines, 28 tests): StepExecutor trait with 3 methods. SyntheticStepExecutor for testing, PolicyPrepareStepExecutor for real gate evaluation (DONE). TxExecutionEngine<E: StepExecutor> orchestrates full lifecycle but is NOT used by the CLI.
- plan.rs (7,760 lines, 123 tests): Core types solid. Gate evaluation real via TxPreparePolicyAuthorizer/TxPrepareApprovalChecker/TxPrepareTargetLookup traits with StorageBacked impls. StepAction enum (line 529) has SendText, WaitFor, AcquireLock, etc.
- tx_idempotency.rs (2,041 lines, 68 tests): Strong implementation. Integrated in TxExecutionEngine.
- mission_loop.rs (5,291 lines, 116 tests): Real evaluation loop but dispatch is not connected.
- tx_observability.rs (1,287 lines, 20 tests): Event taxonomy complete, integrated in TxExecutionEngine.

# Critical CLI Finding (pass 3)
Both ft tx run AND ft robot tx run import:
  tx_prepare_gate_inputs_allow_all as build_robot_tx_prepare_gate_inputs
This means the CLI BYPASSES all policy gates despite SnowyHorse's PolicyPrepareStepExecutor work. The CLI also doesn't use TxExecutionEngine — it manually calls evaluate_prepare_phase / execute_commit_phase with synthetic inputs. The ft-y9lnb.6 bead addresses this.

# What's Done
- Policy gate wiring (ft-y9lnb.2, closed): prepare-phase traits implemented with StorageBacked adapters.

 {"id":"ft-y9lnb.1","title":"Implement PaneStepExecutor: connect tx step execution to Robot Mode send/wait-for primitives","description":"# Goal
Create a PaneStepExecutor that implements the StepExecutor trait by dispatching step operations to actual panes via WeztermInterface.

# Critical Design Constraint: Sync Trait Inside Async Runtime
The StepExecutor trait is SYNC (returns Vec<T>). But the entire CLI runs inside rt.block_on(async { ... }) (main.rs line 16248). WeztermInterface methods are async (return Pin<Box<dyn Future>>). Calling block_on() inside an existing block_on() panics (nested runtime).

## Solution: Approach A (RECOMMENDED) — Make TxExecutionEngine async-capable
Add a parallel async trait:

    pub trait AsyncStepExecutor {
        async fn evaluate_gates(&self, contract: &MissionTxContract, now_ms: i64) -> Vec<TxPrepareGateInput>;
        async fn execute_steps(&self, contract: &MissionTxContract, fail_step: Option<&str>, now_ms: i64) -> Vec<TxCommitStepInput>;
        async fn execute_compensations(&self, commit_report: &TxCommitReport, fail_for_step: Option<&str>, now_ms: i64) -> Vec<TxCompensationStepInput>;
    }

Then either:
- Add TxExecutionEngine::execute_async() that takes AsyncStepExecutor, OR
- Make TxExecutionEngine generic over both sync and async executors via a unified trait with async fn

PaneStepExecutor implements AsyncStepExecutor. It can .await WeztermInterface calls directly since the CLI is already in an async context.

SyntheticStepExecutor and PolicyPrepareStepExecutor keep the sync trait. A blanket impl wraps sync executors into async ones.

## Alternative: Approach B — Use tokio::task::spawn_blocking
Call the sync StepExecutor methods from a blocking thread that CAN create a new runtime:
    let result = tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || {
        let rt = RuntimeBuilder::current_thread().build().unwrap();
        rt.block_on(async { handle.send_text(pane_id, &text).await })
    }).await;

This works but adds thread-hop overhead per step. Acceptable for the MVP.

## RECOMMENDATION
Start with Approach B (spawn_blocking) for the MVP — it's simpler and doesn't require changing TxExecutionEngine. Then migrate to Approach A in a follow-up bead if performance matters.

# WeztermInterface methods (verified)

- send_text_with_options(pane_id, text, no_paste, no_newline) -> WeztermFuture<()>

# Design: PaneStepExecutor
## evaluate_gates(): delegate to PolicyPrepareStepExecutor (sync, no I/O, no nesting issue)

## execute_steps(): iterate contract.plan.steps, for each TxStep match on action:
- SendText { pane_id, text, paste_mode } -> spawn_blocking + block_on(handle.send_text(...))
- WaitFor { pane_id, condition, timeout_ms } -> spawn_blocking + block_on(poll get_text until match or timeout)
- StoreData { key, value } -> StorageHandle (sync, no nesting issue)
- AcquireLock/ReleaseLock -> PaneWorkflowLockManager if available
- Unsupported (RunWorkflow, NestedPlan, Custom, etc.) -> success: false, reason_code: "unsupported_action"
- On fail_step match: inject deterministic failure

## execute_compensations(): same dispatch but iterating contract.plan.compensations

# Unit Tests (12 minimum)
1. execute_steps SendText happy path: 3 steps, MockWezterm send_text succeed -> 3 success inputs
2. execute_steps SendText pane not found: send_text error -> failed input
3. execute_steps WaitFor match: get_text returns matching text -> success
4. execute_steps WaitFor timeout: pattern not found in time -> failed input
5. execute_steps StoreData: key/value stored -> success
6. execute_steps unsupported action: RunWorkflow -> failed with unsupported_action
7. execute_steps fail_step injection: forced failure at named step
8. execute_steps mixed: 3 steps, step 2 fails -> step 3 not executed
9. execute_compensations happy: compensation text sent -> success
10. execute_compensations failure: compensation send fails -> failed
11. execute_compensations empty: no compensations -> empty vec
12. evaluate_gates delegates: same results as PolicyPrepareStepExecutor

NOTE ON TESTS: Unit tests use MockWezterm which is NOT async — mock_wezterm_handle() returns synchronous mocks. Tests can use the simpler approach. The spawn_blocking path is only needed in the production code path where real WeztermInterface is genuinely async.

# Structured Logging

- tracing::warn! on failure with error context
- tracing::instrument on execute_steps and execute_compensations

4. Works when called from inside rt.block_on(async { ... }) (the CLI context).

+    // The two synthetic test cases pin the load-bearing claim of the

+    // (the case that requires a CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED bump and
+    // an atomic redeploy) corrupt the decoder's positional alignment

+    /// ft-kuxho.B.2: a strictly-additive future field appended to a PDU's
+    /// frame must be consumed-but-ignored by the canonical decoder. The
+    /// `check_compat` helper from ft-kuxho.B.1 is invoked with a
+    /// simulated (encoder=v+1, decoder=v) pair to confirm the window
+    /// agrees on `v` for this case before the decode attempt.
+    #[test]
+    fn cross_version_additive_end_of_struct_decodes_canonically() {
+        // (a) Compat window: simulated v+1 encoder, v decoder; both
+        // sides advertise CODEC_VERSION as their minimum. The window
+        // overlap is [v, v+1] → [v, v] = {v}; agreed = v.
+        let decision = check_compat(
+            CODEC_VERSION + 1,        // encoder local
+            CODEC_VERSION,            // encoder local_min
+            CODEC_VERSION,            // decoder remote (== our current)
+            CODEC_VERSION,            // decoder remote_min
+        )
+        .expect("v+1 vs v with min=v must be compat-window-compatible");
+        assert_eq!(
+            decision,
+            CompatDecision::Compatible {
+                agreed: CODEC_VERSION
+            },
+            "v+1 vs v must agree on v (older peer dictates dialect)"
+        );
+
+        // (b) Encode a PDU with multiple fields (better stress for the
+        // positional decoder than a single-field PDU). SetLayoutCycle
+        // has a Vec<String> tail field which is the realistic shape of
+        // a "field added at the end of a struct".
+        let pdu = Pdu::SetLayoutCycle(SetLayoutCycle {
+            tab_id: 9,
+            layout_names: vec!["main".to_string(), "split".to_string()],
+        });
+        let mut encoded = Vec::new();
+        pdu.encode_with_mode(&mut encoded, 0xCAFE, CompressionMode::Never)
+            .expect("canonical encode must succeed");
+
+        // (c) Append simulated future-field bytes after the canonical
+        // frame. The existing tail-padded property is the load-bearing
+        // claim — re-asserted here in version-pair language.
+        for tail in [
+            vec![0x42_u8, 0x42, 0x42, 0x42], // 4-byte synthetic future field
+            vec![0x00_u8],                    // single null tail byte
+            vec![0xFF_u8; 32],               // 32-byte synthetic future blob
+        ] {
+            let mut framed = encoded.clone();
+            framed.extend_from_slice(&tail);

+            let decoded = Pdu::decode(framed.as_slice()).unwrap_or_else(|e| {

+                    tail.len()

+                "cross-version additive-end decode produced different PDU (tail={} bytes)",
+                tail.len()
+            );
+            assert_eq!(

+                "cross-version additive-end decode lost serial (tail={} bytes)",
